title: Fibonacci Number
statement: The Fibonacci numbers, commonly denoted `F(n)` form a sequence, called the Fibonacci sequence, such that each number is the sum of the two preceding ones, starting from 0 and 1. Given `n`, calculate `F(n)`.

bruteForce.description: Use standard recursion: `fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)` without caching. (Slow, O(2^N) time).

optimized.description: Use recursion with memoization, or compute iteratively in O(N) time and O(1) space by maintaining the last two Fibonacci values in variables.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Base case: F(0) = 0 and F(1) = 1.
Seed the variables for F(0) and F(1).
Iteratively calculate next state while sliding values forward.

cppExplanation.explanation: Base cases: F(0)=0, F(1)=1.
Initialize preceding Fibonacci state values.
Step forward to compute next Fibonacci value.

timeComplexity: O(N) - Single loop running from 2 to N.
spaceComplexity: O(1) - Constant auxiliary space is used.
followUps: Can you write it using actual recursion with memoization? (Yes, use a cache map/decorator to store results and achieve O(N) time)
commonMistakes: Using plain recursion without memoization (causes exponential time complexity).

title: Power of Three
statement: Given an integer `n`, return `true` if it is a power of three. Otherwise, return `false`. An integer `n` is a power of three if there exists an integer `x` such that `n == 3^x`.

bruteForce.description: Iteratively multiply 1 by 3 until it exceeds or equals n.

optimized.description: Use recursion. Base cases: if `n <= 0`, return false. If `n == 1`, return true. If `n` is not divisible by 3, return false. Otherwise, recurse with `n // 3`.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Base case: numbers less than or equal to 0 cannot be powers of 3.
Base case: 3^0 = 1, so 1 is a power of 3.
If n is not divisible by 3, it cannot be a power of 3.
Recursively divide n by 3 and repeat.

cppExplanation.explanation: Negative numbers and zero are invalid.
3^0 matches 1.
Remainder check: must be divisible by 3.
Recursively divide and call.

timeComplexity: O(log3 N) - We divide n by 3 in each recursive step.
edgeCases: n is a large prime number
n is a power of nine (also power of three)
followUps: Can you solve it in O(1) time and space without recursion or loops? (Yes, check if the maximum power of three fitting in an integer, 1162261467, is divisible by n)
visualization:
27 -> divide 3 -> 9 -> divide 3 -> 3 -> divide 3 -> 1 (Returns True)
28 -> 28 % 3 != 0 -> Returns False
commonMistakes: Forgetting to check `n <= 0`, which can cause infinite recursion or incorrect results.
whenNotToUse: If recursion is forbidden or stack depth is restricted, check if 1162261467 % n == 0 for O(1) time.

title: Range Sum of BST
statement: Given the root node of a binary search tree and two integers `low` and `high`, return the sum of values of all nodes with a value in the inclusive range `[low, high]`.

bruteForce.description: Traverse all nodes using standard DFS, checking every value against range bounds.

optimized.description: Use recursion. Since it is a BST, we can prune subtrees: 1. If node is Null, return 0. 2. If `node.val` is smaller than `low`, prune left child (only search right). 3. If `node.val` is larger than `high`, prune right child (only search left). 4. If in-range, return `val + sum(left) + sum(right)`.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Base case: empty tree adds 0 to sum.

pythonExplanation.line: if root.val < low: return rangeSumBST(root.right, low, high)
pythonExplanation.explanation: If current node is smaller than low, all left subtree elements are also smaller (prune left, search only right).
If current node is larger than high, prune right, search only left.
If current node is in-range, include it and sum both left and right subtrees.

cppExplanation.explanation: Null node check.

cppExplanation.line: if (root->val < low) return rangeSumBST(root->right, low, high);
cppExplanation.explanation: Pruning: ignore left subtree branch since values are too small.
Current value is inside range bounds. Sum it and recurse into both subtrees.

timeComplexity: O(N) - In the worst case, we visit all N nodes in the tree.
spaceComplexity: O(H) - Space used by the recursion call stack, where H is the height of the tree.
dryRun: At Node 10: 7 <= 10 <= 15 -> in range. Sum = 10 + Call(5) + Call(15).
At Node 5: 5 < 7 -> too small. Prune left. Call(Node 5's right child) -> Null -> returns 0.
At Node 15: 15 <= 15 -> in range. Sum = 15 + Call(Null) + Call(Null) = 15.
edgeCases: low and high boundaries match node values
followUps: Can you solve it iteratively? (Yes, using a stack to process node pointers, implementing pruning)
visualization:
10 [Range: 7-15] -> Keep 10. Recurse both.
      /  \
     5    15            -> 5 is < 7 (prune left child). Search right.
          /             -> 15 is in range. Keep 15.
  Sum = 10 + 15 = 25
commonMistakes: Not taking advantage of BST properties (searching both left and right subtrees unconditionally, increasing complexity to O(N) always).

title: Pow(x, n)
statement: Implement `pow(x, n)`, which calculates `x` raised to the power `n` (i.e., `x^n`). Solve it in O(log N) time.

bruteForce.description: Multiply x by itself n times in a loop. (Slow, O(N) time).

optimized.description: Use binary exponentiation. Base case: `n == 0` returns 1. Handle negative power `n` by computing `1 / pow(x, -n)`. In each recursive step: compute `half = pow(x, n // 2)`. If `n` is even, return `half * half`. If `n` is odd, return `x * half * half`.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Any number raised to power 0 is 1.0.
Handle negative powers: x^(-n) = 1 / x^n.
Recursive step: divide the power in half (Binary exponentiation).
If power is even, result is half^2. If odd, result is x * half^2.

cppExplanation.explanation: Base case of power 0.

cppExplanation.line: long long N = n; return 1.0 / myPow(x, -N);
cppExplanation.explanation: Avoid overflow edge case in C++: negating INT_MIN exceeds INT_MAX. Use long long.
Recursive binary divide-and-conquer.

timeComplexity: O(log N) - We halve the exponent value in each recursive step.
spaceComplexity: O(log N) - Stack frames used for recursion depth.
followUps: Can you solve it iteratively in O(1) space? (Yes, using a while loop and bitwise shift updates)
visualization:
2^10 = (2^5) * (2^5) = 32 * 32 = 1024
2^5  = 2 * (2^2) * (2^2) = 2 * 4 * 4 = 32
Binary exponentiation cuts steps from N to log(N).
commonMistakes: Failing to handle `n = INT_MIN` in C++, which overflows when negated because `|INT_MIN| > INT_MAX`.

title: K-th Symbol in Grammar
statement: We build a table of `n` rows (1-indexed). We start with `0` in row 1. In each subsequent row, we replace each occurrence of `0` with `01`, and each occurrence of `1` with `10`. Given row `n` and index `k`, return the `k`-th character. (k is 1-indexed).

bruteForce.description: Generate the entire string row-by-row and index the K-th character. (Slow, causes Out of Memory).

optimized.description: Notice that the first half of row `N` is identical to row `N-1`. The second half is the binary negation of row `N-1`. Calculate the midpoint `mid = 2^(N-2)`. If `k <= mid`, the answer is `kthGrammar(n-1, k)`. If `k > mid`, the answer is the negation `1 - kthGrammar(n-1, k - mid)`.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Base case: Row 1 always starts with 0.
Find the midpoint of the current row (which has size 2^(n-1)).
If K is in the left half, it is identical to row n-1 at index K.
If K is in the right half, it is the binary negation of row n-1 at index (K - mid).

cppExplanation.explanation: Row 1 base value.
Bitwise shift to compute midpoint of the current row size dynamically.
If index is in the right half, recurse and negate result.

timeComplexity: O(N) - Recursion depth goes up to N steps.
dryRun: Step 1: n = 3, k = 3. mid = 2^(3-2) = 2. k(3) > mid(2). Recurse: 1 - kthGrammar(2, 3-2=1).
Step 2: n = 2, k = 1. mid = 2^(2-2) = 1. k(1) <= mid(1). Recurse: kthGrammar(1, 1).
edgeCases: N is large (midpoint calculation must not overflow)
followUps: Can you solve it in O(1) time and space? (Yes, index k-1 has value equal to the number of 1s in its binary representation modulo 2, count bits using popcount)
visualization:
Row 1: 0
Row 2: 0 | 1           (second half is inverse of first)
Row 3: 0 1 | 1 0         (second half is inverse of first)
Find index K by dividing and negating if in right half.
commonMistakes: Attempting to compute row strings, which grows exponentially: row 30 has 1 billion characters, causing Out of Memory.

title: Decode String
statement: Given an encoded string, return its decoded string. The encoding rule is: `k[encoded_string]`, where the `encoded_string` inside the square brackets is being repeated exactly `k` times. `k` is guaranteed to be a positive integer.

bruteForce.description: Scan and replace characters manually using string matching. (Fails on nested brackets).

optimized.description: Use recursion. Maintain a global index pointer `i`. Traverse the string: if character is digit, accumulate the number multiplier. If character is `'['`, recurse to parse the inner string. If character is `']'`, return the current decoded string. Else, append character to the result.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Parse multi-digit multipliers correctly (e.g. 100[a]).
On encountering '[', recursively parse the nested string starting from index i+1.
Multiply the inner decoded string by the number count accumulated.
On encountering ']', return the accumulated string and the current index pointer to the parent caller.

cppExplanation.explanation: Assemble multi-digit numbers.
Recurse on nested subproblems inside brackets.
Return current string block when closing bracket is reached.

timeComplexity: O(N) - We visit each character in the string of size N exactly once.
spaceComplexity: O(N) - Call stack depth is proportional to the number of nested brackets.
dryRun: idx = 0: num = 3. idx = 1: '[' -> calls recurse. idx = 2: 'a'. idx = 3: ']' -> returns 'a'.
Pasted: 3 * 'a' = 'aaa'. num reset. idx = 4: num = 2. idx = 5: '[' -> calls recurse.
followUps: Can you solve it iteratively? (Yes, using two stacks: one for numbers, one for strings)
visualization:
s = '3[a2[c]]'
  DFS Level 1: num=3 -> '[' -> call DFS Level 2
  DFS Level 2: string='a', num=2 -> '[' -> call DFS Level 3
  DFS Level 3: string='c' -> ']' -> returns 'c' -> Level 2 builds 'a' + 2*'c' = 'acc' -> returns 'acc'
  Level 1 builds 3 * 'acc' = 'accaccacc'
commonMistakes: Not resetting the multiplier variable `num` to 0 after popping bracket results, repeating multipliers incorrectly.

title: Special Binary String
statement: Special binary strings are binary strings that satisfy: 1. The number of 0's is equal to the number of 1's. 2. Every prefix of the binary string has at least as many 1's as 0's. You can swap two consecutive, non-empty, special substrings. Return the lexicographically largest resulting string.

bruteForce.description: Generate all possible swap combinations of substrings recursively. (Extremely slow).

optimized.description: Every special string must start with '1' and end with '0'. Split the string into its independent outer special sub-strings. Recursively solve each inner substring `s[1:-1]`, wrap them back in `'1' + solved_inner + '0'`, sort them in descending order (greedy choice), and join them.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Maintain balance: +1 for 1, -1 for 0.
When count reaches 0, we found a closed special substring segment.
Recursively process the inner portion of the special string, and wrap it back in 1 and 0.
Sort the special substrings in descending lexicographical order (greedy choice to maximize value).

cppExplanation.explanation: Maintain prefix balance counts.
Extract, recurse and wrap nested components.
Sort substrings in descending order to form lexicographically largest representation.

timeComplexity: O(N^2) - Substring splitting and recursive calls on string lengths, plus sorting step per recursion layer.
spaceComplexity: O(N) - Memory to store sub-segments and stack frames.
edgeCases: Single special string (cannot be partitioned further)
followUps: Can you explain connection to parentheses matching? (Yes, 1 represents '(' and 0 represents ')'. Special binary strings are valid parentheses listings. The problem is equivalent to finding the lexicographically largest valid parentheses arrangement)
visualization:
s = '11011000' (Parentheses: '(( ) ( ( ) ) )')
  - Sub-segments: '10' and '1100'
  - Sort: '1100' > '10' -> Swap consecutive blocks
  - Output: '11100100'
commonMistakes: Not understanding that every special string must start with '1' and end with '0', missing the outer wrapping boundaries.

title: Parse Lisp Expression
statement: You are given a string expression representing a Lisp-like expression to return the integer value of. The syntax supports three commands: `let` (variable binding), `add` (addition), and `mult` (multiplication).

bruteForce.description: Use regex splitting to extract arguments. (Fails due to nested structures).

optimized.description: Use recursion. Maintain a stack of scopes (variable-to-value maps). A helper function `evaluate` parses expressions: 1. If it's a number or variable, return its value from the active scope. 2. Otherwise, tokenize the expression inside parentheses. Identify the command (`let`, `add`, `mult`). Recurse on arguments: `add` returns `arg1 + arg2`, `mult` returns `arg1 * arg2`, and `let` updates the scope map iteratively.

pythonExplanation.explanation: Maintain a scope stack to support variable shadowing in nested let expressions.
Custom tokenizer that splits tokens by space while keeping parentheses groupings intact.
Push a copy of the parent scope for the local let bindings.
Pop the scope when exiting the let expression to restore parent bindings.

cppExplanation.explanation: Stack of scope maps for variable namespace shadowing.
Push new scope scope as copy of current active scope.
Pop local scope variables when returning from block.

timeComplexity: O(N) - The string expression is parsed, tokenized, and evaluated linearly.
spaceComplexity: O(N) - Storage for the stack of scopes and recursion stack.
edgeCases: Nested let statements shadowing same variables
followUps: Can you explain variable shadowing? (Yes, a variable defined in an inner scope obscures the variable with same name in parent scopes)
visualization:
(let x 2 (add x 3))   ===> Bind x=2
                      ===> Evaluate (add x 3) = 2 + 3 = 5
Scope stack manages namespaces cleanly.
commonMistakes: Incorrectly tokenizing nested expressions (e.g. splitting space inside a nested `(add 1 2)` which breaks arguments indices).

title: Number of Atoms
statement: Given a chemical formula representation string, return the count of all atoms. The formula supports nested parentheses and multipliers, e.g., `H2O` or `Mg(OH)2`.
pattern: DFS Parser Stack / Formula Parsing Stack

bruteForce.description: Use regex matches to find elements and multiply counts in loops. (Fails on nested brackets).

optimized.description: Use recursion or a stack of hash maps. Maintain index pointer `i`. Traverse the formula: if we find an element (starts with uppercase), parse its name and optional count. If `'('`, push a new map to the stack. If `')'`, parse the multiplier after it, pop the top map, multiply all its atoms counts, and add them back to the parent map.

pythonCode:
import collections
def countOfAtoms(formula):
    n = len(formula)
    stack = [collections.defaultdict(int)]
    i = 0
    
    while i < n:
        if formula[i] == '(':
            stack.append(collections.defaultdict(int))
            i += 1
        elif formula[i] == ')':
            i += 1
            # Parse multiplier
            start = i
            while i < n and formula[i].isdigit():
                i += 1
            mult = int(formula[start:i] or 1)
            
            # Pop top map and merge with parent
            top = stack.pop()
            for elem, val in top.items():
                stack[-1][elem] += val * mult
        else:
            # Parse Element Name
            start = i
            i += 1
            while i < n and formula[i].islower():
                i += 1
            elem = formula[start:i]
            
            # Parse Element Count
            start = i
            while i < n and formula[i].isdigit():
                i += 1
            count = int(formula[start:i] or 1)
            
            stack[-1][elem] += count
            
    # Format output alphabetically
    res = stack[0]
    sorted_keys = sorted(res.keys())
    return ''.join(key + (str(res[key]) if res[key] > 1 else '') for key in sorted_keys)

pythonExplanation.explanation: Maintain a stack of hash maps to store atom counts at each parenthesis nesting level.
Read the group multiplier after closing parenthesis. Default is 1 if omitted.
Multiply counts of all popped atoms by the multiplier and merge them into the parent scope.
Return the final formatted counts sorted alphabetically.

cppExplanation.explanation: Use a map inside vector to keep atom names sorted alphabetically naturally.
Pop the nested parenthesis scope.
Build sorted chemical result string omitting count if it equals 1.

timeComplexity: O(N log N) - N is formula length. Parsing takes O(N). Sorting the output elements alphabetically takes O(U log U) where U is number of unique atoms.
spaceComplexity: O(N) - Storage for the stack of maps.
edgeCases: Double digit element names (e.g. He, Mg)
followUps: Can you write it using actual DFS recursion? (Yes, the stack logic maps directly to recursion stack frames)
commonMistakes: Omitting element counts when parsing multi-digit numbers (e.g., parsing H12 as H1 and 2).
